ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for tandoori tiger prawns; FOODON_03311737 (shrimp food product) is the closest parent class covering marinated grilled prawn preparations.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CJW covers shrimps and prawns as a category; no specific code exists for tandoori-marinated tiger prawns. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 227144007 covers prawn as a food substance; no specific concept for tandoori preparation exists.

serving.description: 2 pieces (approximately 2 large tiger prawns with marinade)
serving.natural_language_portion: One serving consists of 2 large tiger prawns (approximately 120 g total including marinade coating), typically served with mint chutney on the side.

nutrient_source.title: Tandoori Prawn / Grilled Marinated Prawn — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods and USDA FoodData Central (Shrimp, cooked, moist heat, FDC ID 175180) used in combination for marinade-adjusted estimation
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition) and USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for tandoori tiger prawns exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN cooked prawn data (Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, 2017 edition, entry: Prawn, cooked) and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 175180 (Shrimp, cooked). A modest upward adjustment of approximately 10–15% for fat and energy has been applied to account for the yogurt-and-oil tandoori marinade. Protein is minimally affected by the marinade. Sodium is elevated due to spice marinade. Values represent the marinated grilled product per 100 g edible portion.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of protein-dominant seafood and shellfish — analogous estimate based on cooked shrimp/prawn data; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for tandoori tiger prawns. Prawns and shrimp are high-protein, very low-carbohydrate foods. The University of Sydney GI Database and the 2008 International Tables (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008) do not list a specific GI for prawns, consistent with the convention that very low-carbohydrate protein foods are assigned a GI of approximately 5 or effectively negligible. GI is estimated at 5 (low) based on the negligible carbohydrate content (~3.2 g/100 g, predominantly from marinade spices and yogurt). Glycemic load per 120 g serving = (5 × 3.84 g available carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 0.2, rounded to 0.2.

hypertension.key_factor: Elevated sodium from tandoori spice marinade
hypertension.guidance: Each 120 g serving provides approximately 624 mg sodium; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and request reduced-salt marinade preparation where possible, keeping total daily sodium below 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content and negligible glycemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only approximately 3.8 g of carbohydrate per 120 g serving and a glycemic load of 0.2, tandoori tiger prawns have minimal impact on postprandial blood glucose and are a suitable high-protein, low-GI choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate dietary cholesterol and sodium; beneficial lean protein and low saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Tandoori tiger prawns provide lean protein with low saturated fat (~1.4 g per serving), but contain moderate cholesterol (~182 mg per serving) and elevated sodium (~624 mg); individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume in moderation and balance with low-sodium accompaniments.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tandoori Tiger Prawns are a classic North Indian seafood preparation in which large tiger prawns (Penaeus monodon) are marinated in a spiced yogurt mixture — typically containing tandoori masala, ginger-garlic paste, red chilli, lemon juice, and oil — and then cooked at high heat in a traditional clay tandoor oven. The result is a smoky, charred exterior with a juicy, tender interior. The dish is commonly served with fresh mint chutney and sliced onions. In Singapore, it is offered at North Indian restaurants such as Bawarchi on South Bridge Road as a starter or side dish. Nutritionally, tandoori tiger prawns are a high-protein, low-carbohydrate food providing approximately 22.5 g protein and only 3.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g, making them suitable for low-GI and high-protein dietary patterns. The fat content is moderate at 4.8 g per 100 g, primarily from the yogurt marinade and cooking oil. Sodium is elevated (~520 mg/100 g) due to the spiced marinade, which is a consideration for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is negligible (estimated GI ~5), and the glycemic load per serving is approximately 0.2, making this dish highly suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is naturally gluten-free and halal-compatible. It is rich in selenium, vitamin B12, phosphorus, and iodine from the prawn itself.
common_user_queries: How many calories in tandoori tiger prawns?
Is tandoori prawn high in sodium?
What is the protein content of tandoori prawns?
Is tandoori prawn good for weight loss?

confidence_reason: No direct entry for tandoori tiger prawns exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN cooked prawn data and USDA cooked shrimp data with a marinade-adjustment factor applied. The adaptation is methodologically sound but introduces estimation uncertainty of approximately ±10–15% for energy and fat. Protein values are reliable. GI is an analogous estimate consistent with the very low carbohydrate content of the dish.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN (Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, 2017) cooked prawn entry and USDA FDC ID 175180 (Shrimp, cooked, moist heat), with a marinade adjustment for yogurt and oil contribution. No direct laboratory analysis of this specific restaurant dish is available. 2. Serving size of 120 g is estimated for 2 large tiger prawns with marinade coating; actual restaurant portion may vary. 3. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN or USDA source entries for cooked prawn/shrimp at the level of confidence required. 4. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g; this is confirmed zero per USDA FDC ID 175180 for cooked shrimp with no hydrogenated fat in the marinade. 5. Sodium value (~520 mg/100 g) reflects the marinated product; plain cooked prawn sodium is approximately 148 mg/100 g per USDA, with the remainder attributed to salt in the tandoori marinade. 6. GI is estimated at 5 (low) as this is a high-protein, very low-carbohydrate food; no direct GI measurement for tandoori prawns is available in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. 7. The food_name has been corrected from the input 'Tigher Tandoori Prawns' (apparent typographic error) to 'Tandoori Tiger Prawns' as the canonical English name. 8. Halal status is assumed based on the restaurant context (North Indian Muslim cuisine at Bawarchi); not independently verified. 9. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FDC ID 175180 and ICMR-NIN cooked prawn data; these are approximations. 10. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source databases consulted (ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods 2017; USDA FDC ID 175180).

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected food_name from input typo 'Tigher Tandoori Prawns' to canonical 'Tandoori Tiger Prawns (2 Pieces)'.
Added full multilingual local_names for Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi as required for North Indian cuisine served in Singapore context.
Estimated nutrient values from ICMR-NIN and USDA FDC ID 175180 with documented marinade adjustment.
Set GI to 5 (low) as analogous estimate for very low-carbohydrate high-protein seafood, documented with University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008.
Documented vitamin_d_mcg = -1 with explicit note per sentinel values documentation rule.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases list are fully consistent.
Ensured all ontology match_type values use only the three allowed values (exact, closest_parent, none).
Confirmed gi_category = 'low' is correct for GI = 5 per ≤55 threshold rule.
